1	---
2	name: blueprint-clean
3	description: Post-write blueprint gate. Strips Lean leakage, project history, and verbosity. Validates and inserts missing source quotes from PDFs/TeXs.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch this subagent AFTER a blueprint-writer round and BEFORE dispatching provers.
10	  - Required to ensure blueprint purity (math-only).
11	  - Include `references/**` in its write-domain so it can spawn `reference-retriever` if needed.
12	---
13	
14	# Blueprint Clean Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to read and refine a blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) to ensure it meets strict quality, purity, and citation standards.
17	
18	## Your Tasks
19	
20	1. **Strip Lean Leakage & Project History:**
21	   - Remove any mention of Lean tactics, typeclass notes, or specific Lean implementation strategies.
22	   - Remove project history references like "since iteration N", "our failed route", etc.
23	   - Ensure the blueprint reads as a timeless, standalone mathematical document.
24	2. **Trim Verbosity:**
25	   - Remove useless, overly conversational paragraphs.
26	   - Remove passages that are irrelevant or redundantly discuss things already implemented, unless they serve a mathematical purpose.
27	   - Keep the prose concise and mathematically precise.
28	3. **Enforce Citation Discipline & Add Quotes:**
29	   - Verify every block derived from a reference has a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`.
30	   - **If the quote is missing** but the reference exists in `references/`, you MUST open the actual reference `.pdf` or `.tex` file (using the `.md` index file as a pointer to find the section/page), extract the verbatim quote, and add it to the blueprint.
31	   - **If a reference SHOULD be cited** but isn't mentioned by the writer, and the source is missing from `references/`, spawn a `reference-retriever` to fetch it.
32	4. **Fix LaTeX:**
33	   - Correct basic LaTeX syntax errors.
34	   - Ensure `\uses{}` and `\label{}` cross-references are formatted correctly.
35	
36	## Spawning a Retriever
37	If you identify a missing reference, spawn it using:
38	```bash
39	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name reference-retriever --slug <slug> --directive-file <file> --write-domain 'references/**'
40	```
41	
42	Return your outcome and the path to your report.
43

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}

83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}

94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}

98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).

101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}

113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
137	  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
138	  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
139	  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
140	  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
141	  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
142	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
143	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
144	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
145	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
146	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
147	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
148	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
149	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
150	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
151	\end{definition}
152	
153	\begin{definition}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
157	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
159	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
160	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
161	  % affine patches" (read from
162	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
164	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
165	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
166	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
167	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). `minorDet d r I J : R^I` is the
168	  %   determinant of the `d × d` submatrix of `universalMatrix d r I` whose
169	  %   columns are indexed by `J`, reindexed to `Fin d` via the order
170	  %   isomorphism `Fin d ≃o J` coming from `#J = d`:
171	  %   `(universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J) |>.det`.
172	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
173	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
174	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
175	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
176	  \[
177	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
178	  \]
179	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
180	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
181	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
182	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
183	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	\leanok
189	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
190	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
192	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
193	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
194	  % affine patches" (read from
195	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
196	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
197	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
198	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
199	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
200	  %   `universalMinor d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
201	  %   is the `J`-minor of `universalMatrix d r I` base-changed along the
202	  %   structure map `R^I → R^I_J = Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`:
203	  %   `((universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J)).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
204	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
205	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
206	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
207	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
208	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
209	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
210	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
211	\end{definition}
212	
213	\begin{lemma}
214	
215	\leanok
216	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
217	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
219	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
220	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
221	  % affine patches" (read from
222	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
223	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
224	  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
225	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
226	  %  on $U^I_J$."
227	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
228	  %   `isUnit_det_universalMinor d r I J : IsUnit (universalMinor d r I J).det`.
229	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
230	  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
231	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
232	  \[
233	    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
234	  \]
235	\end{lemma}
236	\begin{proof}
237	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
238	  \leanok
239	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_minor}, \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of \(P^I_J\)
240	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
241	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
242	  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
243	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
244	\end{proof}

246	\begin{definition}
247	
248	\leanok
249	[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
250	  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
252	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
253	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
254	  % affine patches" (read from
255	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
256	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
257	  % "the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
258	  %  on $U^I_J$."
259	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
260	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
261	  %   `:= (universalMinor d r I J)⁻¹` (the Mathlib nonsingular inverse).
262	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
263	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
264	  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
265	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
266	\end{definition}
267	
268	\begin{lemma}
269	
270	\leanok
271	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
272	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
274	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
275	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). A conjunction (or pair of
276	  %   lemmas):
277	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J * universalMinor d r I J = 1` and
278	  %   `universalMinor d r I J * universalMinorInv d r I J = 1`.
279	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
280	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
281	  genuine two-sided inverse:
282	  \[
283	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
284	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
285	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
286	  \]

293	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
294	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
295	\end{proof}

297	\begin{definition}
298

301	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
303	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
304	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
305	  % affine patches" (read from
306	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "The images of the variables
309	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
310	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
311	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).

318	  \]

320	  universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) base-changed to

328	\begin{definition}
329	
330	\leanok

344	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Via the universal property of the
345	  %   polynomial chart ring `R^J := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ J}) ℤ`,
346	  %   `transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →ₐ[ℤ] Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`

359	\begin{lemma}
360	
361	\leanok
362	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
363	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}

365	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
366	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
367	  % affine patches" (read from
368	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
370	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
371	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
372	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
373	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
374	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
376	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
377	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
378	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
379	  \[
380	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
381	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
382	  \]
383	  which is a unit.
384	\end{lemma}
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
387	  \leanok
388	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
389	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
390	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
391	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
392	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
393	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
394	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
395	\end{proof}

405	  % affine patches" (read from
406	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
407	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
408	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
409	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
410	  %  defined as follows. ...
411	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
412	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
413	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
414	  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
415	  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
416	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
417	  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
418	  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
419	  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
420	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
421	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
422	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
423	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
424	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
425	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
426	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
427	  \[
428	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
429	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
430	  \]

433	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
434	\end{definition}

439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]

443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
448	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}

468	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
469	
470	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
471	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
472	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Column submatrix of a product]
478	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
480	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
481	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
482	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
483	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
484	  the right factor:
485	  \[
486	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
487	  \]
488	\end{lemma}
489	\begin{proof}
490	
491	\leanok
492	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
493	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
494	\end{proof}
495	
496	\begin{lemma}
497	
498	\leanok
499	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
500	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
502	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
503	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
504	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
505	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
506	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
507	  \[
508	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
509	  \]
510	\end{lemma}
511	\begin{proof}
512	
513	\leanok
514	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
515	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
516	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
517	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
518	\end{proof}
519	
520	\begin{lemma}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
524	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
526	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
527	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
528	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
529	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	
533	\leanok
534	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
535	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}
539	
540	\leanok
541	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
542	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
544	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
545	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
546	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
547	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
548	  \[
549	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
550	  \]
551	\end{lemma}
552	\begin{proof}
553	
554	\leanok
555	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
556	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
557	\end{proof}
558	
559	\begin{lemma}
560	
561	\leanok
562	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
563	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
566	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
567	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
568	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
569	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
570	  defining the chart.)
571	\end{lemma}
572	\begin{proof}
573	
574	\leanok
575	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
576	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
577	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
578	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
579	\end{proof}
580	
581	\begin{lemma}
582	
583	\leanok
584	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
585	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
587	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
588	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
589	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
590	  \[
591	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
592	  \]
593	\end{lemma}
594	\begin{proof}
595	
596	\leanok
597	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
598	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
599	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
600	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
601	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}
605	
606	\leanok
607	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
608	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
610	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
611	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
612	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
613	  \[
614	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
615	  \]
616	\end{lemma}
617	\begin{proof}
618	
619	\leanok
620	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
621	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
622	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
623	\end{proof}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}
626	
627	\leanok
628	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
629	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
631	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
632	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
633	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
634	  the image matrix:
635	  \[
636	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
637	  \]
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	
641	\leanok
642	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
643	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
644	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
645	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
646	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
648	\end{proof}
649	
650	\begin{lemma}
651	
652	\leanok
653	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
654	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
655	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
656	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
657	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
658	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
659	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
660	  \[
661	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
662	  \]
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	
666	\leanok
667	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
668	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
669	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
670	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
671	\end{proof}
672	
673	\begin{lemma}
674	
675	\leanok
676	[Naturality of the image matrix]
677	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
679	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
680	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
681	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
682	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
683	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
684	  \[
685	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
686	  \]
687	\end{lemma}
688	\begin{proof}
689	
690	\leanok
691	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
692	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
693	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
694	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
695	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
696	  Proved directly in Lean.
697	\end{proof}

699	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
700	
701	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
702	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
703	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
704	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
705	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
706	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
707	\[
708	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
709	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
710	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
711	\]
712	
713	\begin{definition}
714	
715	\leanok
716	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
717	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
718	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
719	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
720	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
721	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
722	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
723	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
724	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
725	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
726	\end{definition}
727	
728	\begin{definition}
729	
730	\leanok
731	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
732	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
733	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
734	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
735	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
736	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
737	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
738	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
739	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
740	\end{definition}
741	
742	\begin{lemma}
743	
744	\leanok
745	[Left inclusion lies over \(R\)]
746	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
748	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_left}
749	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left})
750	  intertwines the two structure maps:
751	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/x]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
752	\end{lemma}
753	\begin{proof}
754	
755	\leanok
756	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
757	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
758	\end{proof}
759	
760	\begin{lemma}
761	
762	\leanok
763	[Right inclusion lies over \(R\)]
764	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
765	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
766	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_right}
767	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right})
768	  intertwines the two structure maps:
769	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/y]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
770	\end{lemma}
771	\begin{proof}
772	
773	\leanok
774	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
775	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
776	\end{proof}
777	
778	\begin{lemma}
779	
780	\leanok
781	[Left factor is a unit in the double localisation]
782	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
783	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
784	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
785	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(x\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
786	\end{lemma}
787	\begin{proof}
788	
789	\leanok
790	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
791	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
792	  Proved directly in Lean.
793	\end{proof}
794	
795	\begin{lemma}
796	
797	\leanok
798	[Right factor is a unit in the double localisation]
799	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
801	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
802	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(y\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
803	\end{lemma}
804	\begin{proof}
805	
806	\leanok
807	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
808	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
809	  Proved directly in Lean.
810	\end{proof}
811	
812	\begin{lemma}
813	
814	\leanok
815	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
816	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
818	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, def:gr_image_matrix}
819	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
820	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
821	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
822	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
823	  \[
824	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
825	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)
826	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot P^A_C ,
827	  \]
828	  a product of two units once \(P^A_C\) is inverted in \(D\).
829	\end{lemma}
830	\begin{proof}
831	
832	\leanok
833	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet},
834	  \(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C) = \det(M_C)\) with \(M = (X^A_B)^{-1} X^A\); split
835	  the \(C\)-minor of the product (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and the
836	  determinant as \(\det((X^A_B)^{-1}) \cdot P^A_C\). The first factor is a unit
837	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}); the second becomes a unit under
838	  \(\iota\) by hypothesis. Proved directly in Lean.
839	\end{proof}
840	
841	\begin{definition}
842	
843	\leanok
844	[Localised transition map \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J \to S_I\)]
845	  \label{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}
846	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleΘIJ}
847	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right, def:gr_minor_det}
848	  The triple-overlap transition map
849	  \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)] \to S_I = R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]\):
850	  the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) along the doubly
851	  inverted minor \(P^J_I P^J_K\) of the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
852	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) post-composed with the inclusion
853	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}\) into \(S_I\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}). It is
854	  well-defined because both inverted minors map to units of \(S_I\):
855	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\) by \cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit} (then
856	  \cref{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}), and \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K)\)
857	  by \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross} (with cross factor
858	  \(P^I_K\) a unit, \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}).
859	\end{definition}
860	
861	\begin{definition}
862	
863	\leanok
864	[Localised transition map \(\Theta_{J,K} : S_K \to S_J\)]
865	  \label{def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk}
866	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleΘJK}
867	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, def:gr_minor_det}
868	  The triple-overlap transition map
869	  \(\Theta_{J,K} : S_K = R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)] \to S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]\):
870	  the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
